l3_raw:
**Findings:**  
Cardiomegaly with tortuous, ectatic thoracic and abdominal aorta. Aortic calcifications noted along descending aorta. No focal pulmonary consolidation or pneumothorax identified. Small bilateral pleural effusions present as blunting of costophrenic angles; right greater than left. Left basilar atelectasis versus infiltrate cannot be definitively excluded due to superimposition over mediastinal structures. Abdomen unremarkable except for multiple surgical clips projecting anteriorly near midline abdomen (likely cholecystectomy). Multiple ECG leads overlain patient body without evidence of acute cardiopulmonary abnormality beyond findings described above.

**Impression:**  
No active pneumonia detected. Cardiomegaly consistent with chronic changes including possible prior heart failure treatment. Tortuosity/ectasia of ascending and descending thoracoabdominal aortas likely age-related degenerative change. Possible small-to-moderate volume loss from scarring/fibrosis within lingula/left lower lobe lung base which may represent postobstructive collapse/infiltration pattern but not definitive diagnosis given limited visualization through adjacent opacities obscuring evaluation. Right-sided basal opacity could reflect subsegmental atelectasis vs early interstitial edema depending upon comparison studies if available clinically correlated.

**Findings:**  
No focal airspace disease or pleural effusion identified within visualized lung fields. Cardiomediastinal silhouette is normal size and contour, with no acute pulmonary edema pattern noted. Thoracic spine shows degenerative changes consistent with age-related wear-and-tear. No pneumothorax detected. Calcified granulomas present bilaterally at apices; likely represent prior infection (e.g., tuberculosis). Left upper lobe nodule measures approximately 8 mm × 6 mm without evidence of growth over time compared to previous imaging from two years ago when it was smaller (~4–5 mm), suggesting possible benign etiology such as scarring rather than malignancy. Right lower rib fracture appears healed peripherally along anterior cortex but may be subacute given its location relative to adjacent ribs—could reflect trauma versus spontaneous healing depending on patient history. Abdomen demonstrates multiple calcifications projecting through diaphragm into left hemidiaphragmatic region, most suggestive of splenic infarct/infected abscess sequelae. Vascular ectasia visible throughout thoracoabdominal vasculature.

**Impression:**  
Left basilar opacity represents probable post-inflammatory fibrosis/scarring. Small right basal opacities also suggest chronic inflammatory process. Normal heart size and mediastinum. Pulmonary nodules appear stable since last exam despite slight increase in apparent diameter due to magnification effect near clavicle borders — unlikely malignant transformation unless accompanied by interval enlargement beyond measurement error margins (<20% change would raise concern) or associated suspicious features like spiculation/calcification/hypodensity indicating necrotizing pneumonia/infiltrate/mass lesion requiring further evaluation if clinically indicated. Multiple abdominal calcific densities compatible with old infectious/surgical sequela including potential splenic pathology. Degenerative spinal changes limited to thoracic vertebrae only.

l3_raw:
**Findings:** Normal heart size and pulmonary vascularity. No focal airspace disease, pleural effusion or pneumothorax. Right upper lobe nodule measuring approximately 8 mm with no suspicious features (e.g., spiculation). Degenerative changes of thoracic spine bilaterally without acute fracture. Left shoulder dislocation from prior trauma. Multiple right-sided rib fractures consistent with old injury; one lateral anterior fragment remains displaced compared to initial imaging but may represent healing rather than new displacement given its small size relative to adjacent bone fragments. Electrocardiogram leads overlying lungs.

**Impression:** Clear lung fields. Small calcified granuloma within the right upper lobe likely representing an infectious etiology such as tuberculosis. Old healed bilateral rib fractures. Unusual appearance at left base which could be artifact versus early infiltrate — recommend follow-up if clinically indicated. Thoracolumbar degeneration. Prior traumatic scapular subluxation/displacement.

l3_raw:
**Findings:**  
Right basilar airspace disease with adjacent pleural effusion and/or atelectasis; left hemidiaphragm elevation consistent with gastric bubble, likely due to subdiaphragmatic free air or postoperative changes. No pneumothorax identified. Cardiomediastinal silhouette within normal limits. Thoracic spine shows degenerative changes without acute fracture. Surgical clips overlying right upper quadrant suggest prior cholecystectomy. Calcified granulomas noted near anterior inferior rib heads bilaterally. Left shoulder joint demonstrates chronic dislocation of humeral head into glenoid fossa. Right acromioclavicular arthropathy present.  

**Impression:**  
Small-to-moderate sized hiatal hernia containing stomach contents. Bibasilar opacities representing combination of infiltrates/airspace consolidation versus compressive atelectasis secondary to volume loss from lung collapse following recent surgery (right lower lobe wedge resection). Chronic findings include thoracic spondylosis, bilateral calcific tendinopathy of shoulders/claviculae, surgical clips indicating previous abdominal procedures, and probable old tuberculosis sequelae given location relative to scapular tuberosity.

l3_raw:
**Findings:**  
Cardiomegaly with tortuous, calcified thoracic aorta and ectatic pulmonary vasculature suggestive of chronic heart failure or vascular disease. Bibasilar airspace opacities consistent with atelectasis versus infiltrate; small bilateral pleural effusions may be present without definite pneumothorax. Surgical clips overlying right upper lung suggest prior surgery (e.g., cholecystectomy). No acute bony abnormalities identified.

**Impression:**  
Bibasilar patchy opacity likely representing subsegmental atelectasis given low inspiratory effort but cannot exclude early pneumonia superimposed upon underlying fibrosis/interstitial changes from COPD/emphysema. Small left-sided costophrenic angle blunting could represent fluid overload-related edema rather than true effusion due to limited inspiration. Right apical surgical scar suspicious for resection of granulomatous lesion such as TB or fungal infection—further evaluation recommended if clinically indicated. Cardiopulmonary findings compatible with decompensated congestive cardiac failure.

**Findings:**  
No acute cardiopulmonary abnormality identified. Aortic calcification and tortuosity noted without evidence of aneurysm or dissection. No pleural effusion, pneumothorax, focal consolidation, pulmonary nodules, masses, interstitial infiltrates, or airspace disease seen. Right costophrenic angle blunting may represent chronic scarring versus small residual right-sided pleural fluid; follow-up imaging recommended if clinically indicated to exclude evolving pathology. Mild degenerative changes at both knees with anterior osteophytes visible through posterior soft tissues bilaterally. Lumbar spine hardware consistent prior spinal fusion surgery. Left shoulder joint shows posttraumatic deformities suggestive of healed fracture. Degenerative changes within left glenohumeral joint including subchondral sclerosis and marginal osteophyte formation. Calcified granuloma projecting over lateral upper lung apex likely represents old infectious process (e.g., tuberculosis).  

**Impression:**  
Normal heart size and clear lungs. Probable benign finding representing either scar tissue from previous infection/foreign body reaction vs. possible early metastatic adenocarcinoma given patient’s age and history—clinical correlation advised as appropriate. Post-traumatic findings limited by superimposed bony structures but no active injury evident.

**Findings:**  
Cardiomegaly with pulmonary vascular congestion and interstitial edema, left basilar airspace disease consistent atelectasis or infiltrate. No pneumothorax identified. Right internal jugular central venous catheter tip overlying right atrium. Left shoulder surgical changes compatible prior discectomy/arthroplasty. Degenerative spinal degeneration noted throughout thoracic spine. Multiple calcified granulomas within upper lobes bilaterally likely representing old healed TB infection.

**Impression:**  
Left lower lobe airspace opacity may represent pneumonia versus post-obstructive alveolar consolidation secondary to bronchovascular crowding from cardiopulmonary compromise. Cardiogenic pulmonary edema is present but less prominent than initial study; consider repeat imaging if clinically indicated given low lung volumes limiting evaluation of small pleural effusions. Prior CT scan performed two weeks ago showed no acute findings suggesting resolution of previously documented infectious process (likely tuberculosis). Surgical clips projecting anteriorly near midline suggest recent abdominal surgery without evidence of free air under diaphragm. Calcific densities along lateral aspect of neck suggestive of costochondral osteophytes adjacent to sternum possibly related to previous rib fractures treated surgically three years earlier.
